skill.name: pytm
skill.description: Guide AI agents through Python-based threat modeling with pytm. It covers STRIDE analysis, trust boundaries, data flow diagrams, reports, and CI integration.
skill.summary: Create threat models as code with pytm and STRIDE.
skill.author: AgentSecOps

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, credential, C2, and weak-crypto patterns, but review shows most are markdown examples, security taxonomy terms, or reference links. No prompt injection or malicious intent was found in SKILL.md. The remaining risk is legitimate guidance that includes package installation and sudo commands, so publication should include a command-execution warning.

medium_findings.title: Privileged Package Installation Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill includes package installation commands, including sudo apt-get for graphviz in CI and troubleshooting examples. This is legitimate setup guidance, but users should review privileged package manager commands before running them.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sudo commands are explicit and would run with elevated privileges if copied by a user. Context shows dependency installation, not malicious escalation, so the risk is moderate.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Command Execution
low_findings.description: Many static external-command findings are caused by fenced markdown examples and inline command text. They document pytm usage and do not execute automatically when the skill is loaded.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged backticks are markdown code block delimiters around Python, YAML, and shell examples. No executable script file or automatic invocation is present.

low_findings.title: Reference URLs Misclassified as Network Behavior
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are reference links to pytm, OWASP, Microsoft STRIDE, MITRE ATT&CK, and NIST material. No evidence found that the skill sends data to these URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear in metadata references and a References section. There are no HTTP client calls, webhooks, or data upload instructions in the reviewed file.

low_findings.title: Security Terms Misclassified as Sensitive or Malicious Content
low_findings.description: Static hits for Windows SAM, C2, and weak cryptography are false positives from security framework names and example threat descriptions. OWASP SAMM and SOC2 are compliance references, not credential access or command-and-control behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious strings are used in threat modeling and compliance context. No evidence found of credential extraction, C2 infrastructure, or cryptographic implementation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Copy-Paste Shell Commands in Documentation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill provides shell commands for installing dependencies, running threat models, and testing graphviz. These commands are expected for this workflow, but agents should ask before executing them in a user environment.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell commands are present and operational if copied. They support dependency setup and report generation rather than stealth or exfiltration.

content.value_statement: Security teams need repeatable threat models that stay aligned with changing architecture. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code guide pytm-based STRIDE analysis and report generation.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to define actors, boundaries, components, and data flows in pytm.
Guides STRIDE review of spoofing, tampering, repudiation, disclosure, denial, and privilege threats.
Shows patterns for web applications, microservices, trust boundaries, and custom threats.
Provides examples for generating diagrams, reports, and mitigation tracking outputs.
Describes CI/CD integration for regenerating and checking threat models.
limitations: It is a guidance-only skill and does not include the referenced helper scripts in the file tree.
Users must install pytm and graphviz before running examples.
Generated threats depend on accurate architecture properties and manual validation.
Threat reports may expose architecture details and require access control.

use_cases.title: Model a Web Application
use_cases.description: Create a pytm model for a web, application, and database tier with trust boundaries and encrypted flows.

use_cases.title: Review Microservice Architecture
use_cases.description: Map API gateways, services, databases, and cross-boundary flows to identify STRIDE threats early.

use_cases.title: Add Threat Modeling to CI
use_cases.description: Use automated checks to regenerate diagrams and identify threats that lack documented mitigations.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Basic Threat Model
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a new application.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a pytm threat model for a simple web application. Include actors, trust boundaries, components, and key data flows.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze STRIDE Threats
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my pytm model for STRIDE coverage. Identify missing properties, weak assumptions, and threats that need mitigations.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a CI Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a CI workflow that runs my pytm threat model, stores diagrams, and flags threats without documented mitigations.

prompt_templates.title: Create Custom Threat Logic
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me define custom pytm threats for my cloud architecture. Include conditions, mitigations, scoring, and review criteria.

output_examples.input: A user asks for a threat model for a three-tier web application.
output: A component list covering user, load balancer, web server, application server, and database.
Trust boundaries for internet, DMZ, and internal network.
STRIDE review notes for authentication, encryption, SQL access, logging, and authorization.

output_examples.input: A team wants to add threat modeling to pull requests.
output: A CI plan that installs required dependencies, runs the model, and uploads diagrams.
A mitigation gate that flags threats with missing acceptance criteria.
A recommendation to review privileged install commands before enabling the workflow.

output_examples.input: An architect needs custom threats for cloud storage and APIs.
output: Custom threat descriptions for exposed storage and API rate-limit abuse.
Suggested conditions based on component properties.
Mitigation guidance for encryption, bucket policy, rate limiting, and key rotation.

best_practices: Use placeholders for secrets and sensitive architecture details in models.
Review generated STRIDE findings with engineers who understand the system.
Protect generated diagrams and reports because they reveal security architecture.
anti_patterns: Do not copy privileged install commands into production systems without review.
Do not treat generated threats as complete without validating model properties.
Do not publish threat reports that expose internal boundaries or sensitive data flows.

faq.question: What does this skill help me build?
faq.answer: It helps create and review pytm threat models using STRIDE, trust boundaries, data flows, and mitigations.

faq.question: Does it run pytm automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. Users decide when to install dependencies or run local commands.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill includes shell and sudo installation examples. They are legitimate but require user review before execution.

faq.question: Can it replace a security review?
faq.answer: No. It supports structured analysis, but qualified reviewers must validate threats, controls, and residual risk.

faq.question: Are the referenced helper scripts included?
faq.answer: No evidence found in the file structure. The available file is SKILL.md, so script references are guidance only.
